documentation.abstract: The species is estimated to have a small population of 200–400 mature individuals, divided between two main subpopulations in Amani Nature Reserve and Nilo Forest Reserve, with a possible remnant population in the Derema Corridor and other small, unprotected forest patches between the two reserves. The largest subpopulation is estimated to comprise 100–200 mature individuals. An ongoing decline is inferred from observed territory abandonment driven by habitat loss and degradation. It is therefore considered to have a very high risk of extinction, and is therefore listed as Endangered.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation and Research Actions Underway

The BirdLife International project staff in Tanzania are working on a collaborative project with a Species Guardian to conserve endemic and threatened biodiversity in the East Usambaras, focusing on this species, through the BirdLife Preventing Extinctions programme. As part of this programme, the following actions are underway, delivered by a team of local staff: (1) Extensive surveys have been carried out in the Amani Nature Reserve and its immediate vicinity, and have been extended towards the eastern and northern parts of the East Usambaras to cover the Derema Corridor as well as the Ngua and Ndola areas. Each known territory of the Long-billed Forest-warbler is being monitored every two months. (2) Implementation of an education and awareness scheme for local landowners and other key stakeholders is in development. Educational materials including posters, calendars and leaflets are being developed in both English and Swahili. (3) Work is currently underway to identify key conservation areas for the species and to prioritise these sites for establishing or trialling forest protection and restoration management agreements. After 4 years, the species has been recorded in forest edge farm agreements, established through the project, which are working to improve edge habitat quality for the species (A. Ward-Francis in litt. 2016). In 2016, the key action to be developed was a plan for removal of the invasive tree, Maesopsis, working with private land owners in order to underpin the wider application of successful interventions (A. Ward-Francis in litt. 2016).

Conservation and Research Actions Proposed

Evaluate the effectiveness of selected forest-management techniques that could benefit this species (Baker and Baker 2001, Cordeiro 2000). Consider methods to reduce disturbance in and around tea plantations (Cordeiro 2000). Conduct ecological surveys to understand why it is apparently so rare and thinly distributed (Baker and Baker 2001). Test techniques to control the expansion of Maesopsis eminii in indigenous forest and to restore heavily invaded forest tracts (L. Borghesio in litt. 2012). Engage stakeholders on the Amani Plateau in forest management and raise awareness of this species's status. Sensitize local farmers to conserve patches of bushy and regenerating vegetation in farms located along forest edges in Amani, Derema and Nilo.
documentation.habitatAndEcology:
In the East Usambaras, its primary habitat is forest edge and large canopy gaps (Cordeiro 2000). It has been found in submontane and montane forest (Baker and Baker 2001), chiefly in open forest edge-type habitat with a high density of vines and climbers (Cordeiro 2000, McEntree et al. 2005) such as clearings and drainage lines (Ryan et al. 1998), in addition to glades on the edge of, or deep inside forest (Cordeiro 2000, McEntree et al. 2005, Borghesio unpubl. data). It can also be found in degraded forest (Baker and Baker 2001, Kyonjola and Njilima 2010), in habitats buffering tea plantations (e.g. secondary Lantana growth and Eucalypt plantations) (Cordeiro 2000, Borghesio unpubl. data), but never far from indigenous forest, as none of >80 currently known territories is more than 100 m from forest edge (Borghesio unpubl. data). Areas that are too frequently disturbed by humans, such as agricultural fields that are burned or weeded regularly, are clearly not favoured (Cordeiro 2000).

Its preference for early successional habitats may mean it is dependent on some form of recurrent disturbance to create forest gaps (Borghesio et al. 2008). It is often absent from large areas of apparently suitable habitat. It forages alone but can sometimes be found in mixed-species parties in the lower and middle storeys (up to 18 m) (Cordeiro 2000, Borghesio unpublished data), preferring the tangled vegetation nearer the ground where it searches foliage for invertebrates (Cordeiro 1998) avoiding sunlit places (Urban et al. 1997, McEntree et al. 2005). It is apparently territorial; one probable male colour-ringed in 2000 has maintained the same territory at the headquarters of Amani Nature Reserve up to 2007 (Cordeiro unpubl. data). Its breeding ecology remains poorly known (Urban et al. 1997).
documentation.population: Recent surveys in the Amani Nature Forest Reserve (ANFR) and Nilo Nature Forest Reserve (NNFR) estimate the population at 100–200 pairs, or 200–400 mature individuals, across both sites (IPP Media 2017). This represents an increase from the previous estimate of 150-200 mature individuals, which was based solely on data from the ANFR (Cordeiro et al. 2001). These findings are consistent with subsequent surveys, conducted after the original estimate, which recorded additional individuals and territories, suggesting a larger population than initially estimated (Cordeiro et al. 2001, Borghesio et al. 2008, Cordeiro et al. 2008, Ojija et al. 2019). Previous indications of extreme rarity were likely in part due to the difficulty in observing the species, even when using playback (Borghesio et al. 2008).
documentation.populationTrend:
The species is inferred to be declining due to habitat loss and degradation. While it tolerates some disturbance, occurring in powerline cuts within forests and gaps dominated by the exotic shrub Lantana camara adjacent to forest (Borghesio et al. 2008), it is rare in degraded forests and agricultural mosaics (Cordeiro et al. 2008). Since 2000, it has been recorded in only five of 16 small forest fragments, including the Derema Corridor (Borghesio et al. 2008, Cordeiro, Mkongewa and Borghesio unpublished data). Illegal gold mining in NNFR, intensifying since 2013, has degraded forest edges and streams, leading to territory abandonment in chronically disturbed areas (IPP Media 2017).

Tree cover within the species' range has declined by 8.1% over the past decade, with greater loss in the ANFR, where forest integrity is low to moderate, than in the NNFR, which retains mostly high integrity forest (Hansen et al. 2013, Grantham et al. 2020, Global Forest Watch 2025). Multiple previously known territories have been lost due to rapid conversion of unprotected forests in tea plantations for cash crops and chainsaw logging (Cordeiro and Mkongewa unpublished data). Additionally, remote areas of protected forests, including in the ANFR, face significant threats from illegal tree cutting, which escalated in 2023 (Anonymous pers. comm.). Additionally, unquantified fine-scale habitat loss and degradation may be impacting the species more than the observed rate of tree cover remote sensing data suggest. While natural disturbances, such as landslides and treefalls, create the successional habitats needed by the species (Borghesio et al. 2008), ongoing deforestation and habitat conversion exceed the tolerated frequency of disturbance, causing declines in suitable habitat and abandonment of territories, ultimately leading to population decline.

Additionally, the spread of Maesopsis eminii likely hinders recolonisation of regrowth areas, as sites with 45% canopy cover failed to support recolonisation (Kyonjola 2013). The abundance of M. eminii has been shown to negatively correlate with the species' distribution, with a predicted 45% decline in detection at invaded sites (Borghesio 2012). While natural regeneration in disturbance-free areas allows 50% of restored plots to recolonise after four years, the ongoing spread of M. eminii could exacerbate habitat degradation, restricting recolonisation and further threatening population recovery and range expansion.

The rate of population decline is uncertain. The population was previously suspected to be as high as 400-500 individuals between 2016–2018, though this estimate remains uncertain. The current population is estimated at 200–400 mature individuals, but the extent of the decline and the rate at which it occurred are unknown.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Artisornis moreaui and A. sousae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as A. moreaui following Dowsett & Forbes-Watson (1993) and Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).
documentation.threats:
Despite favouring some open forest-edge habitats, Long-billed Forest-warbler remains vulnerable to forest destruction, especially as it may not cross open spaces easily (Urban et al. 1997). There is heavy pressure on most unprotected forest and along forest edges, especially for mining, pole-cutting, cultivation and firewood-collection (Evans 1997, Kessy 1998, Doggart et al. 2004). As this forest becomes depleted, pressure on smaller forest reserves is likely to increase (Evans 1997, Kessy 1998). In addition, the harvesting of plants for medicinal purposes may cause some disturbance, clearance of vegetation along powerlines has resulted in the loss of some territories. An increasing threat is the spread of the invasive tree Maesopsis eminii, which is replacing natural vegetation, including climbers and vines, in forest gaps preferred by A. moreaui. This species alters the forest structure, converting naturally dynamic mosaics into dense, uniform stands that not preferred by A. moreaui (Ojija et al. 2019). Having a montane distribution that is close to the maximum altitude within its range, this species is potentially susceptible to climate change (BirdLife International unpublished data). Habitat loss has recently accelerated, with unprotected forests in tea plantation areas rapidly converted for cash crops and logging. Illegal tree-cutting has also escalated in remote parts of protected forests, including Amani Nature Reserve, with a sharp increase reported in 2023 (multiple individuals, pers. comm.).

Conservation efforts have helped protect Nilo Forest Reserve from illegal activities to some extent, but several threats persist. Farmers continue to encroach on the reserve by establishing cardamom, clove, and cinnamon farms, while other crops such as bananas, cassava, maize, and beans are grown just metres from the forest boundary. Incidents of illegal logging, fuelwood collection, and pole cutting have also been recorded, though these remain low due to intensive patrols and heightened awareness among local communities (Ojija et al. 2019). The primary disturbance inside the reserve comes from fuelwood collection, as local people living nearby rely on firewood as their main energy source for cooking and heating. Additional disturbances, including fire incidents, pit sawing, agricultural encroachments, mining activities, and the presence of stumps were also observed during habitat surveys (Ojija et al. 2019).

Despite reports of intensifying illegal gold mining in the Nilo Forest Reserve (IPP Media 2017), mining in northern Amani Nature Forest Reserve declined from 23.9% in 2000 to 12% in 2023 following stricter enforcement against illegal activities. However, open forest also decreased from 35.6% to 14.4% over the same period, primarily due to forest succession, as regenerating areas transitioned into closed forest (Msoma et al. 2024). Thus, even in well-protected areas where threats like mining and agriculture are mitigated following conservation efforts, natural succession into closed forest can still alter habitat suitability.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

geographicRange.narrative: This species occurs in the Nilo Nature Forest Reserve (NNFR) and the Amani Nature Forest Reserve (ANFR) in the East Usambara Mountains in Tanzania (Cordeiro 1998, Cordeiro et al. 2001). Additionally, small, potentially remnant populations occur within unprotected forest fragments just to the north of ANFR and in the Derema Corridor (Cordeiro et al. unpublished data).

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is described as generally uncommon, although common in the Amur and Ussuri river valleys and common during its non-breeding season in South-East Asia and the Indian Subcontinent (del Hoyo et al. 2006). National population sizes have been estimated at < c.1,000 individuals on migration in Korea and c.100-10,000 breeding pairs and c.50-1,000 individuals on migration in Russia (Brazil 2009). The population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat destruction.
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat destruction.

documentation.abstract:
Mediterranean regional assessment: Near Threatened (NTº)

Short-eared Owl has a restricted breeding range within the Mediterranean, with breeding only occurring in Spain, where there is a population size of 320-340 mature individuals. The breeding extent of occurrence is only 6,421 km2 but the number of locations is not known, the population is not thought to be severely fragmented, and there is no evidence for a decline in the population within Spain. It could potentially be inferred that there has been a decline in the quality of habitat as satellite imagery shows much of the area within the breeding range is used for agriculture. The population does fluctuate, but it is not certain whether such fluctuations would represent a severe fluctuation per IUCN Red List guidelines. Therefore, based on these data the species could qualify as Vulnerable under criterion D1, and would nearly qualify for Vulnerable under criterion B1b(iii)c(iv), so would warrant a listing as Near Threatened under this criterion. This is a migratory species, and the wider European population is stable (BirdLife International 2021), thus there is opportunity for a rescue effect for this population. Therefore, a regional adjustment of one category down is made to the assessment, and the final assessment of the species is Near Threatened under criterion D1.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

CITES Appendix II. Bern Convention Appendix II. EU Birds Directive Annex I. The protection of nesting cover for waterfowl and reclaimed and replanted strip-mines and dyke land, which provide nesting and foraging cover has indirectly benefited this species (Olsen et al. 2013).

Conservation Actions Proposed

Large continuous tracts of habitat should be maintained (Olsen et al. 2013); importantly marshlands and moorlands need to be protected from destruction, drainage and afforestation and agricultural habitats such as meadows and old pasturelands should be managed for conservation (Tucker and Heath 1994). Other management suggestions have included establishing a standardized survey protocol, monitoring predation and human disturbance and public education and research (Olsen et al. 2013).
documentation.habitatAndEcology: In Europe, this species breeds mainly on moorland, marshes and bogs, in newly cleared forest and young evergreen plantations, in uncultivated grassland and in steppe (Olsen et al. 2013). It breeds between late march and June and generally lays seven to ten eggs (König 2008). It nests on the ground on a dry site, sometimes in wetter situation and female shows tendency to build nest (almost unique behaviour among Strigidae) (Olsen et al. 2013). It feeds mostly on small mammals but also other small vertebrates and insects (König 2008), but usually relies on rodent populations which can determine its spatial distribution and nesting success (Hagemejer and Blair 1997). In the north of its range in Europe it is thought to be highly migratory although migration may be confused with nomadic food searches and juvenile dispersal. Wintering areas may also become breeding areas if food is plentiful (Olsen et al. 2013).
documentation.population: The breeding population in Spain is estimated at 160-170 pairs (320-340 mature individuals) with a fluctuating trend (see BirdLife International 2021). It is unknown whether these are extreme fluctuations per IUCN Red List Guidelines, but in the absence of evidence of a decline, a stable population is suspected.
documentation.threats: Due to its nesting habits and nomadic nature, the species is vulnerable to habitat loss at any season; conversion of open habitats to agriculture, grazing, recreation, housing and resort development is a key factor in decline, as well as reforestation in some areas. Wind-turbine developments may also impact the species. In central Europe declines have been caused by drainage and the intensification of agriculture, together with persecution, rodenticide poisoning, urbanization and traffic mortality. It does not always occupy apparently suitable habitat, which may be due to levels of prey or predation or the effects of distribution and abundance. Domestic and feral cats and dogs cause disturbance (Olsen et al. 2013).
documentation.useAndTrade: There is some illegal shooting of the species (see Brochet et al. 2016).

geographicRange.narrative: In the Mediterranean region, the only breeding population is found in north-western Spain.

documentation.abstract: The Jamaican owl (Asio grammicus) is a medium-sized owl that is endemic to the island of Jamaica.

Mediterranean regional assessment: Least Concern (LC)

This species has an extremely large range (its extent of occurrence (EOO) is much larger than 20,000 km² and its area of occupancy (AOO) is much larger than 2,000 km²), and hence it does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criteria (criteria B and D2). The population size is very large, (much larger than 10,000 mature individuals), and hence it does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criteria (criteria C and D). The population size trend is not known, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size reduction criterion (criterion A). The probability of extinction has not been calculated for this species, therefore criterion E cannot be applied. For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

CITES Appendix II. Bern Convention Appendix II.

Conservation Actions Proposed

It is suggested that road collisions could be decreased by reducing the availability of small rodents in close proximity to roads, through allowing vegetation to grow naturally or by planting short bushes (Baudvin 1997). Management to prevent vole declines should involve the creation of a mosaic of different habitat types with grassland mown at different times of the year together with undisturbed strips (Aschwanden et al. 2005). Breeding can be encouraged through the provision of artificial nesting platforms and open fronted nestboxes (Olsen 1999). In addition in harsh winters, food supplies can be augmented through the provision of feeding stations near day roosts (König and Weick 2008). The use of pesticides and rodenticides should be also controlled. Monitoring of population size, trend and threats would be useful, including studies into the incidence of possible harvesting in population dynamics.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: This species breeds in dense vegetation adjacent to open areas such as grassland or shrubland, including open forest, nesting and roosting in forest. In mainland Europe, breeding occurs in edge woodland and scrub. Outside the breeding season it is found in similar habitats, including farmland with hedgerows. The breeding season is from February to July (Olsen 1999). It uses the abandoned stick nests of other species, such as crows (Corvus), magpies (Pica), raptors, herons (Ardea) (König and Weick 2008). It also accepts artificial baskets, and sometimes lays in cavities in trees or cliffs, or on the ground. Clutches are generally five to seven eggs. It feeds principally on small mammals, although some studies have found birds to be important (Olsen 1999). The species is mainly migratory in Fennoscandia and Russia but further south in Europe it is more sedentary (Hagemeijer and Blair 1997).
documentation.population: The population size has not been accurately quantified for the region, but based on the figures in BirdLife International (2021) the population size is likely to significantly exceed the thresholds for consideration as threatened. The population trend is unknown.
documentation.threats: Agricultural intensification and declining vole populations are also driving declines in Europe (Hagemeijer and Blair 1997). Locally, threats include pesticide use, persecution and road traffic collisions (König and Weick 2008). There is evidence of illegal trade of all owl species in North Africa (Brochet et al. 2016, O. Haitham pers. obs.) and there is a risk that individuals may be taken from nest sites for trade (Symes et al. 2015) or because people accidentally think that chicks have been abandoned (Karim Rousselon 2019, unpublished data). The growth and spread of the human population is causing habitat destruction and disturbance in forests because of the use of wood as fuel for cooking and heating, and for the expansion of agricultural fields for crops and livestock (Bergier 1987, Cherkaoui et al. 2009, Tellería et al. 2019). Destruction and disturbance due to intense human use of forests especially threatens tree-nesting raptors like the Northern Long-eared Owl and leads to the abandonment of the breeding territories. In Tunisia fire may be a threat in the forests, but no loss of habitat has been observed (N. Hamdi pers. obs.), and it may be be affected by competition with Pharaoh Eagle-Owl (N. Hamdi pers. obs.).
documentation.useAndTrade: All owl species are illegally traded in North Africa (Brochet et al. 2016) and there are some evidences of limited trapping in Egypt (O. Haitham pers. obs.). In Moroccan popular belief, all nocturnal birds of prey are used for magic rituals.

geographicRange.narrative: This species occurs across the Mediterranean region.

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations). The population size is very large, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population is suspected to number 50,000-499,999 mature individuals (Partners in Flight 2022).
documentation.populationTrend: The population trend has not been investigated. Tree cover within the range is lost at a rate of 15% over three generations (Global Forest Watch 2021, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). Apart from forests, the species is also found in woodland, plantations, thorn scrub and man-made habitats, including urban parks (Arizmendi et al. 2020); consequently, the current rate of tree cover loss may not be driving an equivalent population decline. Nevertheless, the species is sometimes locally killed out of concern that it may prey on domestic chickens (Arizmendi et al. 2020), though the impact on the population size has not been quantified. Tentatively, population declines are here placed in the band 10-19% over three generations.

documentation.abstract: This species has a very large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations). The population size may be moderately small to large, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: Partners in Flight estimated the population to number fewer than 50,000 mature individuals (A. Panjabi in litt. 2008, 2017); thus it is placed in the band 20,000-49,999 mature individuals here.
documentation.populationTrend: The species is undergoing a large, significant decline (Partners in Flight 2019), which is thought to be caused by low levels of habitat loss within the range (Tracewski et al. 2016).

documentation.habitatAndEcology: The species occurs most frequently up to 1,500 m in mesic shrub-steppe receiving 350-450 mm of rain per year (but as little as 180-250 mm in the southern parts of its continental range (S. Imberti in litt. 1999)), with an upper stratum of shrubs and a ground stratum of tussock-grasses (Vuilleumier 1997).
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is described as 'uncommon' (Stotz et al. 1996). The population was once thought to be declining owing to overgrazing of native grasslands in Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego. However, there is no evidence that it occupied these grasslands and its current preferred habitat of temperate shrubland is not threatened and the population is believed to be stable throughout its range (Vuilleumier 1997).
documentation.populationTrend: The population was once thought to be declining owing to overgrazing of native grasslands in Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego. However, there is no evidence that it occupied these grasslands and its current preferred habitat of temperate shrubland is not threatened and the population is believed to be stable throughout its range (Vuilleumier. 1997).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

geographicRange.narrative: The species has an apparently disjunct range: a population in north-west Patagonia (west Neuquén, west Río Negro and west Chubut, Argentina, and adjacent Chile in Ñuble, Malleco, Aysén and Concepción) may be geographically isolated from a southern population inhabiting the Patagonian mainland (south Santa Cruz, Argentina and Magallanes, Chile) and north Tierra del Fuego and Gable Island in the Beagle channel (Collar et al. 1992, Vuilleumier 1997, S. Imberti in litt. 1999). It is apparently partially migratory with birds recorded north to Valparaíso in Chile (Collar et al. 1992).

documentation.abstract: This species has a large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.

This species is considered to have a medium dependency on forest habitat, and tree cover is estimated to have declined by 1.3% within its mapped range over the past 10 years (Global Forest Watch 2022, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). Therefore, as a precautionary measure, it is tentatively suspected that this loss of cover may have led to a decline of between 1-19% in the species' population size over the same time frame.

documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

The species is found in several protected areas throughout its range, including Machu Picchu Historical Sanctuary and Manu National Park in Peru, and Madidi National Park in Bolivia.

Conservation Actions Proposed

Survey to produce an accurate estimate of the population size. Monitor the population trend.

Expand the protected area network to effectively protect key sites. Effectively manage protected areas, utilising emerging opportunities to finance protected area management with the joint aims of reducing carbon emissions and maximizing biodiversity conservation. Incentivise conservation on private lands through expanding market pressures for sound land management and preventing forest clearance on lands unsuitable for agriculture (Soares-Filho et al. 2006).
documentation.habitatAndEcology: The species is mostly found between 2,700 and 3,700 m elevation. Above the timberline, this species occurs in páramo grassland and elfin forest. Below the timberline, it prefers dense undergrowth at the edge of cloud-forest, often with Chusquea bamboo stands; it appears to tolerate considerable habitat disturbance. Its diet consists of arthropods, taken from understorey foliage (del Hoyo et al. 2003, Lloyd 2020).
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is described as common (Lloyd 2020).
documentation.populationTrend: Due to the remoteness and inaccessibility of large parts of its range, the species's habitat is currently not considered under imminent risk. Tree cover loss within the range is negligible (<1% over ten years; Global Forest Watch 2021, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein); moreover, the species appears able to tolerate considerable habitat disturbance (Lloyd 2020). Therefore, in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats, the population is suspected to be stable.
documentation.threats: The primary threat to this species is the loss of its habitat through deforestation (Soares-Filho et al. 2006, Bird et al. 2011). It has probably always had a relatively small population, but it may be susceptible to the impacts of grazing and burning in its Andean timberline habitat (del Hoyo et al. 2003). Nevertheless, tree cover loss is currently negligible within its remote range (<1% over ten years; Global Forest Watch 2021, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein), and the species is not considered under imminent risk.

geographicRange.narrative: Asthenes helleri has a restricted range in the Andes of Cusco and Puno, Peru, south to La Paz, Bolivia.

documentation.abstract: This species has a very large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations). The population size may be moderately small to large, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

It occurs in Los Cardones National Park, Argentina

Conservation Actions Proposed

Quantify the population size. Monitor the population trend. Conduct ecological studies to determine the precise habitat requirements. Investigate its tolerance of habitat degradation and fragmentation. Grant protection to areas of suitable habitat to safeguard against clearance and degradation.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: It is fairly common to uncommon in dense arid montane scrub and open Polylepis and Alnus woodland with grasses and mixed scrub (e.g. Gynoxys and Baccharis), at elevations of 3,000-4,200 m, locally down to 2,500 m (Vuilleumier 1969; Ridgely and Tudor 1994; Parker et al. 1996; Mazar Barnett et al. 1998; T. Hjarsen in litt. 1999; Remsen 2020). A specimen collected at 1,600 m in Bolivia in June 1914 suggests that it may descend in altitude during the winter months (M. Pearman in litt. 2001). It has also been found in tall clumps of Festuca bunchgrass, intermingled with areas of short turf and even patches of bare soil (Mazar Barnett et al. 1998), and shrubbery and hedgerows away from Polylepis (S. Mayer in litt. 1999).
documentation.population:
The species is described as 'fairly common' (Stotz et al. 1996). The population has not been quantified, but based on observational records it is suspected to number 10,000-19,999 mature individuals, though this requires confirmation.

Even though the subpopulation structure has not been assessed, it is here assumed that the species forms at least three moderately large subpopulations (per eBird 2021).
documentation.populationTrend: Population trends have not been directly estimated. Even though the species tolerates habitat disturbance, the population is suspected to be in slow decline locally due to habitat loss and degradation (Remsen 2020). The overall rate of decline is unlikely to exceed 10% over ten years.
documentation.threats: Although not entirely dependent on Polylepis woodlands, their destruction through clearance for cultivation, firewood-collection and burning for pasture undoubtedly threatens some populations (Stattersfield et al. 1998). Pinus and Eucalyptus plantations are replacing suitable native vegetation within the range (T. Hjarsen in litt. 1999). The species nevertheless appears to tolerate at least moderate habitat degradation and is present on cattle pastures and on cultivated land (Remsen 2020).

geographicRange.narrative: Asthenes heterura occurs in central Cochabamba, La Paz, Potosí, Tarija and Chuquisaca, Bolivia, and in Jujuy, Salta, Tucumán and Catamarca, north Argentina (Ridgely and Tudor 1994; Fjeldså and Kessler 1996; Fjeldså and Mayer 1996; Remsen 2020). It has probably been overlooked (Ridgely and Tudor 1994; Fjeldså and Mayer 1996; T. Hjarsen in litt. 1999; S. Mayer in litt. 1999).

Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations). The population size may be small, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

No targeted actions are known.

Conservation Actions Proposed

Conduct surveys to estimate the population size and trend. Monitor the population trend. Research potential threats to get a better understanding of their impacts on the species. Monitor rates of habitat loss.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: It inhabits arid valleys with scattered thorny bush, cacti and trees and shrubs, including Schinus molle, Carica, Tecoma and Spartium (BirdLife International 2000; Schulenberg 2020). It avoids disturbed habitats (Fjeldså and Krabbe 1990; Schulenberg 2020).
documentation.population:
The population is tentatively suspected to number 2,500-9,999 mature individuals based on an assessment of known records, descriptions of abundance and an assumption that only a proportion of its range is occupied.

Based on observational records (eBird 2021), the species is tentatively assessed as forming three subpopulations. The largest subpopulation likely contains more than 1,000 mature individuals, but it may approach this number.
documentation.populationTrend: This population is suspected to be in slow decline owing to ongoing habitat loss and degradation (del Hoyo et al. 2003).
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Asthenes dorbignyi. A. arequipae, A. usheri and A. huancavelicae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as A. dorbignyi following SACC (2005 & updates), Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993) and Stotz et al. (1996).
documentation.threats: Its habitat is thought to be declining as a result of burning and grazing and there may be indirect habitat damage caused by a change in humidity resulting from deforestation of adjacent areas (BirdLife International 2000). Nevertheless, habitat loss is suspected to be slow (Collar et al. 1992; Schulenberg 2020).

geographicRange.narrative: Asthenes huancavelicae occurs in the Andes of west and central Peru (locally in Ancash; an undescribed taxon which may represent a separate species), as well as Huancavelica, Huanuco and Ayacucho.

documentation.abstract: This grassland specialist is suspected to be declining moderately rapidly as a result of ongoing habitat loss and degradation, primarily driven by overgrazing and conversion to agriculture, plantations, pasture, urban areas and wind farms. It is therefore assessed as Near Threatened.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

The species is included in the National Action Plan (PAN) for the Conservation of Birds of the Campos Sulinos (ICMBio 2025).

Conservation Actions Proposed

Conduct surveys to determine the population size and trend. Search for breeding populations outside the humid Pampas (Claramunt et al. 2022). Determine whether this species is a partial migrant (Claramunt et al. 2022). Monitor rates of habitat loss throughout its range. Conduct regular surveys to track population trends. Increase the area of suitable habitat that receives formal protection.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: The species inhabits southern temperate grasslands, including areas with tall wet grass (e.g. Paspalum quadrifarium) and sedges near wetlands, from sea-level to 950 m (del Hoyo et al. 2003). In north-eastern Buenos Aires at least, its preferred habitat is seasonally inundated grassland dominated by Spartina densiflora (L. Pagano and A. Bodrati in litt. 2010). It is also found by roads where its habitat is maintained and is noted to show some tolerance to cattle grazing (L. Pagano and A. Bodrati in litt. 2010). It is insectivorous, reportedly feeding on Lepidoptera larvae, Coleoptera, Pentatomidae (shield-bugs), Orthoptera and Hymenoptera, which it probably gleans from the ground or low vegetation. It breeds during the austral spring and summer, with eggs and nestlings recorded in November-December. The nest is constructed on or near the ground in dense vegetation. Its clutch size is usually three or four eggs, perhaps ranging from two to five (del Hoyo et al. 2003). There is some evidence the species may be at least partially migratory (Claramunt et al. 2022).
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species occurs at relatively low densities compared to other grassland specialists and is described as scarce and uncommon to locally fairly common (del Hoyo et al. 2003, MAyDS and AA 2017, Claramunt et al. 2022). The largest populations are found in the Bahia de Samborombón and the watershed of the Rio Salado in Buenos Aires, Argentina (L. Pagano and A. Bodrati in litt. 2010).
documentation.populationTrend:
The population size of this species is thought to have undergone significant declines in the past, probably occurring mostly during the late 20th century and early 21st century, with models indicating a reduction in distribution of 65% compared to historical levels (MAyDS and AA 2017, Claramunt et al. 2022). It requires patches of short grass within a matrix of tall grass and thus cattle grazing is beneficial to this species to some extent, however loss of suitable habitat is ongoing primarily through overgrazing and conversion to agriculture, exotic forage pastures, urban areas, wind farms and pine and eucalyptus plantations (Isacch and Cardoni 2011, Cardoni et al. 2015, MAyDS and AA 2017, Claramunt et al. 2022, ICMBio 2025, R. A. Dias in litt. 2025, C. Trofino-Falasco in litt. 2025, I. Berkunsky in litt. 2025, P. Grilli in litt. 2025). Over the past decade this has led to local population declines and even extirpation of the species from sites in the range (I. Berkunsky in litt. 2025).

Investigation into land cover change in Argentina suggests that approximately 25% of natural grassland habitat has been lost during the period 2010-2020 (MapBiomas 2025). While rates of habitat loss calculated have not been restricted to the range of the species, the data on transitions of natural grassland show a high degree of overlap with the species’ distribution, and much of this loss has occurred within its mapped range. Based on this, the ongoing threats and evidence of local extirpation from sites in the range, it is suspected that the species is undergoing a moderately rapid decline. To account for uncertainty, the suspected rate of decline is tentatively placed in the range 10-29% over ten years.
documentation.threats:
In the Rio Lujan watershed, Argentina, the species was formerly common; however, since the end of the 1970s all suitable habitat in the area (thousands of hectares) has been lost. In the 250 km between Partido de San Nicolas and Capital Federal, the species has lost 70% or more of its habitat during the last 30 years (L. Pagano and A. Bodrati in litt. 2010). Declines probably occurred mostly during the late 20th century and early 21st century, with models indicating a reduction in distribution of 65% compared to historical levels (MAyDS and AA 2017, Claramunt et al. 2022).

This species is threatened by the ongoing and accelerating loss of suitable grassland through drainage and conversion to agriculture, exotic forage pastures and urban areas, as well as the expansion of wind farms and pine and eucalyptus cultivation (L. Pagano and A. Bodrati in litt. 2010, MYaDS and AA 2017, Claramunt et al. 2022, ICMBio 2025, R. A. Dias in litt. 2025, C. Trofino-Falasco in litt. 2025, I. Berkunsky in litt. 2025, P. Grilli in litt. 2025). It requires patches of short grass within a matrix of tall grass and thus cattle grazing is beneficial to this species to some extent, however habitat is being reduced by overgrazing, burnings, herbicide application and plantation of the salt-tolerant Lotus tenuis (Isacch and Cardoni 2011, Agra et al. 2015, Cardoni et al. 2015, Claramunt et al. 2022, R. A. Dias in litt. 2025, C. Trofino-Falasco in litt. 2025, I. Berkunsky in litt. 2025, P. Grilli in litt. 2025).

documentation.abstract: This restricted range species meets the range size criterion for both the EOO and AOO. Although the impacts of habitat loss/degradation to this species are not well understood, records indicate that the species frequently occurs in highly degraded habitats including roadsides and near inhabited areas, and the population is therefore thought to be stable. It is also highly unlikely that the number of Locations meets or approaches the thresholds for threatened. As the species does not meet or approach the thresholds for any other Red List Criteria, it is assessed as Least Concern.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

None.

Conservation Actions Proposed

Several recommendations are provided by Lloyd (2020), including: conduct research on life history and ecology, conduct comprehensive surveys to determine abundance, occupancy, and distribution, and to identify all population to determine a population size and population trends; study the impacts of threats; conduct a systematic study on the genus to determine the species/subspecies status of various taxa within this complex.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: Occurs in humid Andean elfin forest/upper elevation cloud-forest tree-line habitats, surrounded by patches of paramo grassland, as well as secondary habitats and is often recorded by roadsides; recorded from 3,030-3,710 m (Remsen 2003, eBird 2025).
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is thought to be "fairly common" at a small number of localities (Remsen 2003). Many areas of suitable habitat within the area have not been surveyed, and the range and population may be larger than currently thought (H. Lloyd in litt. 2007).
documentation.populationTrend: The extent of the linear Andean tree-line ecotone has been greatly reduced from land-use change (Remsen 2003), and the impacts of this on the species has not been sufficiently studied. However, the species is still common in highly disturbed habitats (Remsen 2003), being frequently recorded by roadsides and in inhabited areas (eBird 2025), and the population is therefore suspected to be stable.
documentation.threats: The extent of the linear Andean tree-line ecotone has been greatly reduced from land-use change (Remsen 2003), and the impacts of this on the species has not been sufficiently studied. Firewood extraction may also pose a threat (Lloyd 2004). However, the species is still common in highly disturbed habitats (Remsen 2003), being frequently recorded by roadsides and in inhabited areas (eBird 2025).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

geographicRange.narrative: Known only from the Department of Junin, Peru.

documentation.abstract: This species has a very small range, within which it is known from very few locations. Its high elevation habitats are highly restricted and recent evidence suggests that its habitat is declining and being fragmented as a result of illegal cultivation. Consequently it is classified as Endangered.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

The species is considered nationally Endangered in both Colombia (Renjifo et al. 2016) and Venezuela (Sharpe and Lentino 2015). Some of its range is formally protected by Sierra de Perijá National Park, Venezuela, but there is no active management (A. Viloria per J. Fjeldså in litt. 1998; Sharpe and Lentino 2015). In Colombia, it is protected by ProAves's dedicated Chamicero del Perijá Reserve, preserving 750 ha of prime habitat. Cerro Pintado lies to the north of the park, but the inhabitants of the Villanueva village, Colombia, attempt to protect the natural habitat (A. Viloria per J. Fjeldså in litt. 1998).

Conservation Actions Proposed

Estimate the population size (C. J. Sharpe, J-P. Rodríguez and F. Rojas-Suárez in litt. 1999). Determine its global distribution. Study its ecological requirements (C. J. Sharpe, J-P. Rodrígiez and F. Rojas-Suárez in litt. 1999). Assess habitat availability using aerial photographs (C. J. Sharpe, J-P. Rodrígiez and F. Rojas-Suárez in litt. 1999). Evaluate the state of the high-altitude páramo throughout Sierra de Perijá in order to see whether it is necessary to create a strict protected area in Colombia or enforce the existing Sierra de Perijá National Park in Venezuela (C. J. Sharpe in litt. 2015). Manage and effectively protect Sierra de Perijá National Park (Rodríguez and Rojas-Suárez 1995, Sharpe and Lentino 2015). Expand Sierra de Perijá National Park to encompass more of the species's range (Sharpe and Lentino 2015).
documentation.habitatAndEcology: It inhabits subparámo and shrubbery vegetation at elevations of 2,950-3,400 m (Hilty and Brown 1986; A. Viloria per J. Fjeldså in litt. 1998; Hilty 2003; Remsen et al. 2015; Sharpe and Lentino 2015). There have been suggestions that it may favour bamboo (A. Viloria per J. Fjeldså in litt. 1998), but its close relative, the White-chinned Thistletail A. fuliginosa, does not inhabit bamboo stands (F. G. Stiles in litt. 1999). Birds are often seen in pairs, foraging for arthropods and occasionally berries (Fjeldså and Krabbe 1990). Immatures and breeding-condition adults have been recorded in July (Fjeldså and Krabbe 1990).
documentation.population: The population is estimated to number 250-999 individuals based on an assessment of known records, descriptions of abundance and range size. This is consistent with recorded population density estimates for congeners or close relatives with a similar body size, and the fact that only a proportion of the estimated Extent of Occurrence is likely to be occupied. This estimate is equivalent to 167-666 mature individuals, rounded here to 150-700 mature individuals.
documentation.populationTrend: It is suspected that the slow, but ongoing degradation of its habitat through cattle grazing and agriculture is driving a slow population decline (Sharpe and Lentino 2015; Renjifo et al. 2016). The rate of decline has not been quantified, but it is not thought to exceed 10% over ten years.
documentation.threats: Habitat below 2,000 m in the Sierra de Perijá is threatened by narcotics cultivation, uncontrolled colonisation, cattle-ranching and mineral exploitation, which are all facilitated by the many roads approaching the sierra from the Colombian side (C. J. Sharpe in litt. 1997, 2000; A. Viloria per J. Fjeldså in litt. 1998; López-O. et al. 2014; Sharpe and Lentino 2015). The species's high-elevation habitat was thought to be less affected by these developments, but the area of suitable habitat is now believed to be fragmented and declining as a result of illegal cultivation (C. J. Sharpe in litt. 2003) and cattle grazing (López-O. et al. 2014). Only patches of montane forest remain on the steepest slopes of the Cerro Pintado. Forest is still being lost to burning and the cultivation of Papaver. Security problems are making access very difficult (R. Strewe in litt. 2003). Temperature increase and habitat alteration caused by climate change may push the species upslope (Sharpe and Lentino 2015).

geographicRange.narrative: Asthenes perijana only occurs in the Sierra de Perijá (and particularly Cerro Pintado) on the border of Colombia (Guajira) and Venezuela (Zuila) (Meyer de Schauensee and Phelps 1978; Hilty and Brown 1986). In Colombia, it has been observed in July 2008 (López-O. et al. 2014) and regularly since 2014, particularly in the Chamicero del Perijá Reserve (Remsen et al. 2020). There have been no records in Venezuela for several decades (Sharpe and Lentino 2015).

documentation.abstract: This species has a large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is described as 'fairly common' (Stotz et al. 1996). This population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat degradation (del Hoyo et al. 2003).
documentation.populationTrend: This population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat degradation (del Hoyo et al. 2003).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is described as 'common' (Stotz et al. 1996). This population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat degradation (del Hoyo et al. 2003).

documentation.abstract: The streak-backed canastero (Asthenes wyatti) is a species of bird in the Furnariidae family. It is found in Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Venezuela. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical high-altitude grassland. Seven subspecies are recognized:

This montane species is suspected to have a small population, which is confined to several small, isolated subpopulations. The species is feared to be declining owing to ongoing habitat loss and degradation. It is therefore considered to be Near Threatened.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

The species is listed as Near Threatened at the national level in Peru (SERFOR 2018). A population exists in the well-protected Carrasco National Park, Bolivia, and in the Machu Picchu National Sanctuary, Peru (S. K. Herzog in litt. 1999; Remsen 2020).

Conservation Actions Proposed

Conduct surveys of suitable habitats within and surrounding the known range to determine its distribution. Quantify the population size. Monitor the population trend. Protect high-altitude native habitats within the range.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: The species occurs in páramo grassland near the treeline, Polylepis woodland, elfin forest and mossy slopes with scrub and Gynoxis and Ribes bushes at 2,750-4,300 m (Ridgely and Tudor 1994; Parker et al. 1996; Remsen 2020).
documentation.population:
The species is described as 'uncommon' (Stotz et al. 1996). Based on its localised distribution, the population is suspected to number 2,500-9,999 mature individuals, though this requires confirmation.

Even though the subpopulation structure has not been assessed, it is here assumed that the species forms several isolated subpopulations with likely less than 1,000 mature individuals each.
documentation.populationTrend: The species is suspected to be declining, as habitat is lost and degraded through agricultural activities and burning, which is moreover hampering vegetation recovery (Remsen 2020). The rate of decline has not been quantified, but is likely low.
documentation.threats: Although some montane habitats in Peru are still relatively pristine (Fieldjså and Krabbe 1990), in Bolivia native habitats have been extensively degraded by livestock grazing, burning and firewood collection (Dinerstein et al. 1995). Heavy grazing by livestock and the uncontrolled use of fire also combine to prevent Polylepis regeneration, especially where cutting for timber, firewood and charcoal occurs (Fjeldså and Kessler 1996).

geographicRange.narrative: Asthenes urubambensis occurs locally, and probably discontinuously, on the east slope of the Andes. The race huallagae occurs in San Martín, La Libertad, Huánuco and Pasco, north and central Peru, with the nominate race in Cuzco and Puno, south Peru, and La Paz and Cochabamba, west Bolivia (Ridgely and Tudor 1994).

documentation.abstract: This species has a large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). The population trend is not known, but the population is not believed to be decreasing sufficiently rapidly to approach the thresholds under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is described as 'uncommon and patchily distributed' (Stotz et al. 1996). The population trend for this species cannot be determined based on the available information.
documentation.populationTrend: The population trend for this species cannot be determined based on the available information.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). The population trend appears to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified. This population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.populationTrend: This population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Asthenes wyatti (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously split into A. wyatti (SACC [2005 and updates]; Sibley and Monroe [1990, 1993]; Stotz et al. [1996]) and A. sclateri (SACC [2005 and updates]; Sibley and Monroe [1990, 1993]).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.
